description: The right side of the body and, by extension, the right side of anything oriented: the right hand, the right path, the right bank. Body-relative direction, the partner of 'lawe' (left). Names direction only — Phi deliberately does not use this word for correctness or entitlement.
sound_symbolism: The firm 'ku' plants itself, and the bright 'ri' points outward — the steadying hand and the indicating finger. Deliberately shares no sounds with its partner 'lawe' (left), so the two directions can never be confused, spoken or written.
grammatical_notes: A noun naming the side itself: 'mua kuri' (on the right). Before another noun it modifies by position: 'kuri ruela' (the right path). Paired with 'lawe' (left); the pair is intentionally phonetically disjoint. Not used for 'correct' (see 'shewo' true) or for rights/entitlements.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: By refusing the English conflation of 'right' (direction) with 'right' (correct), Phi keeps direction value-neutral: no side is privileged.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Direction reckoned from the body, the way a river is read from its banks and a tool from the hand that holds it.
